frameworks.name: causal perturbation of cortical decision circuits
frameworks.one_line: Reversible inactivation tests whether PPC activity is necessary for decisions.

frameworks.name: sensorimotor decomposition of decision-making
frameworks.one_line: Dissociates sensory-evaluation and motor-planning contributions of PPC.

frameworks.name: perceptual decision-making / visual categorization
frameworks.one_line: Behavioural setting in which PPC activity is probed.

core_question: Does posterior parietal cortex play a causal role in sensory-evaluation aspects of perceptual and categorical decisions, beyond motor planning?
key_assumptions: Reversible inactivation localises causal contribution to the inactivated region.
Decision behaviour can be decomposed into sensory-evaluation and motor-planning components.
Stimulus vs response-target placement in the inactivated visual field separates these components.
manipulations: Visual stimuli placed inside vs outside inactivated visual field.
Motor response targets placed inside vs outside inactivated visual field.
Task type: perceptual visual discrimination and visual categorization.
key_findings: PPC inactivation impaired both sensory-evaluation and motor aspects of decisions.
Impairment was preferentially larger when stimuli rather than response targets were in the inactivated field.
interpretations: PPC plays a causal role in decision-making, not merely a correlative one.
PPC is preferentially involved in evaluating attended task-relevant sensory stimuli rather than motor planning.
